nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, sheltered reef flats, lagoons, and bedrock grooves with moderate to bright light and good water movement; typically found at depths of 2–20 m

decorPreferences: stable rockwork or sandbed for skeletal attachment
position away from direct laminar flow
areas with good water circulation to prevent detritus accumulation

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae housed in the polyp tissue. Has been documented to benefit from supplemental weekly feedings of phytoplankton, microplankton, rotifers, cyclops plankton, or other fine particulate foods suitable for filter-feeding invertebrates. Target feeding via pipette or turkey baster near open polyps is generally recommended.

avoidWith: corals with long sweeper tentacles (e.g., Hammer, Torch, Frogspawn)
aggressive LPS corals in direct contact
filamentous algae and cyanobacteria (can overgrow skeleton)
strong direct laminar flow (damages fragile polyps)

summary: Pipe Organ Coral (Tubipora musica) is an Indo-Pacific octocoral known for its vivid red calcareous skeleton of parallel organ-pipe tubes. Listed on CITES Appendix II, aquacultured specimens are strongly preferred. An intermediate-care species, it is particularly susceptible to algae fouling of its exposed skeleton.
careNotes:
Despite producing a rigid red calcareous skeleton, the Pipe Organ Coral belongs to Order Malacalcyonacea — the octocorals — rather than Scleractinia, the true stony corals. It is the only member of the genus Tubipora and the family Tubiporidae, making it genuinely one of a kind among reef corals. The vivid red color of the skeleton comes from iron salts in the calcium carbonate, not from pigment produced by the polyps. In life, the skeleton is largely hidden beneath soft tissue and expanded polyps; the characteristic red tubes only become visible when the polyps retract.

The skeleton is built from numerous parallel hollow tubes of calcium carbonate that run perpendicular to the substrate and are connected at intervals by horizontal platforms called stolons. Each tube houses a single polyp bearing eight pinnate (feather-like) tentacles, a feature shared by all octocorals. Growth is extremely slow by reef coral standards, and the skeleton depends on stable calcium and alkalinity levels to continue developing — instability in these parameters can stall growth or cause skeletal degradation.

Algae fouling: The primary husbandry challenge specific to Tubipora musica is the propensity for algae — particularly filamentous algae, cyanobacteria, and turf algae — to colonize the exposed calcareous skeleton between polyp tubes. Once established, algae can spread across the skeleton surface and physically block polyps from extending, effectively smothering sections of the colony. Preventive management includes: (1) maintaining good water quality with low nitrate and phosphate, (2) ensuring sufficient water movement across the colony to dislodge settling detritus, (3) active herbivore populations (turbo snails, hermit crabs, urchins), and (4) manual removal of any algae present before it spreads. Placing this coral away from algae-prone areas of the aquarium is generally advised.

Flow: Most hobbyist guidance recommends medium intermittent flow, avoiding direct strong currents that damage the fragile polyps. At the same time, enough circulation to dislodge detritus from the skeletal tubes is important for keeping the skeleton clear. In practice, gentle chaotic or randomized flow (wavemaker-style, not laminar) is generally regarded as the best approach — enough to remove detritus from skeletal crevices without stressing the polyps.

Lighting: Recommended PAR figures vary across sources, ranging from around 75–150 PAR in most practical guidance, with occasional outliers suggesting much higher levels that likely reflect tolerance rather than optimal conditions. The weight of practical experience and scientific literature places this species as a moderate-light coral. Avoid both extremes: too little light causes the zooxanthellae (the symbiotic algae living inside the polyp tissue that provide most of the coral's nutrition through photosynthesis) to underperform; too much risks bleaching of the soft tissue. Acclimate gradually when moving colonies to new lighting conditions.

CITES Appendix II: Tubipora musica is regulated under CITES Appendix II, meaning international trade requires documentation demonstrating the specimen was legally obtained. Aquacultured specimens (frags) are available and strongly preferred, as wild collection has contributed significantly to population declines. The species is considered near-threatened due to harvest pressure for aquariums, jewelry, and traditional medicine.

Skeletal supplements: Regular supplementation of calcium, magnesium, strontium, iodine, and other trace elements is generally recommended to support skeletal integrity. Target calcium around 380–450 ppm and magnesium around 1250–1400 ppm, consistent with a standard mixed-reef parameter regime.

Growth habit: Unlike Green Star Polyps or Xenia, Tubipora musica has a notably slow and contained growth habit — it is not known to spread aggressively across rockwork or crowd neighboring corals. This makes it a more manageable occupant in a mixed reef.
breedingNotes: Captive propagation by fragging (cutting a piece of the colony) is possible but reportedly more challenging than with most soft corals, owing to the rigid calcareous skeleton. Frags are typically secured to rock or rubble with reef-safe adhesive or epoxy and allowed to heal in place. Sexual reproduction in the wild occurs via broadcast spawning; deliberate sexual breeding in captivity is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Pipe Organ Coral (Tubipora musica)
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.025), dKH (8–12), lighting (moderate to high), flow (medium), care level (moderate), and feeding notes. Tank size not specified.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Pipe Organ Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked: PAR 75–100 (lower than ReefChasers 300–350 outlier); calcium ~425 ppm; magnesium ~1350 ppm; alkalinity 8–9 dKH typical. Recommended high flow to remove detritus from skeletal tubes. Notes slow growth rate and crustacean colonization of skeleton. Native range: Pacific, Australia, Indonesia.

additional.name: Reeflings – Organ Pipe Coral (Tubipora musica) Complete Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity SG 1.023–1.026, PAR 50–150 (consistent with moderate interpretation), flow moderate. Care level moderate to easy. Indo-Pacific native range, depth 25 m max. Confirmed zooxanthellae photosynthetic primary nutrition with microplankton supplementation.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory – Organ Pipe Coral (Tubipora musica) Ultimate Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 77–82°F (25–27.8°C, higher than LiveAquaria); colony can reach 12 in high x 24 in across (30 x 60 cm) in aquarium; algae fouling of skeleton documented as primary failure mode; notes chaotic flow preferred but difficult in home aquariums. Indian Ocean native range cited.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Tubipora musica
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Tubiporidae, Order: Malacalcyonacea), native range (Indian Ocean, central-western Pacific, south of Japan, west to East Africa, Red Sea), depth 2–20 m, wild colony up to 3 m across, and CITES near-threatened status. Red skeleton iron-salt pigmentation confirmed.
